chapters.title: If anything can block your rise, it is your ego
chapters.text:
Many people think the outside world is the main barrier to a successful life, but everything outside passes through an internal filter, and the most common enemy of success is a person's ego. Ego is a sense of self-worth and self-importance. It can make you crave outside approval and build a wall between you and your real self.

Early in your career, your ego can block learning and the growth of talent. It can also blind you to success, lower your goals, and push you to accept weak results later. Even when you fail, your ego can magnify each failure and make recovery harder.

At every stage of life, your ego can hold you back. It is the enemy of bold goals, of a calling, of a great passion, of a fresh start. Of course, you can only have aspirations by first believing you can reach them, but that belief should not lean on past wins. It should rest on how self-contained, self-driven, and careful you are. Some people claim that raw talent is all they need, which is not true. You have to keep checking your abilities, since that leaves room to grow.

It is human to feel nervous when starting something, so people often seek comfort outside instead of inside. Shifting your focus to outside approval can lead you to chase goals that are not your own. To tame your ego, pull back from your emotions and do the hard work of self-improvement. It is better to have ambition, a desire to sharpen your craft, than to be eaten by your ego. Read on to see how ego can hurt your success and how to grow ambition instead.

chapters.title: Learn from others and build real relationships
chapters.text:
Humility is one of the steps that breaks the belief that you know it all. The day you stop learning is the day you die. When you keep reminding yourself that you are not the best in the room, you can hold your ego in check.

Lifelong learning starts with admitting you are not perfect. The more you chase perfection, the more you reach. Drop the illusions you carry about yourself by connecting with others, asking for feedback, and offering what you have to the world. Take Kirk Hammett, for example. He passed on the chance to join Metallica at first, knowing he had more to learn. He found a mentor, Joe Satriani, a well-known guitarist. By learning from Satriani, Hammett picked up new skills and grew as a guitarist. Being a mentee can help you keep your ego in line. When feedback comes in, the ego tends to brush off the harsh parts by telling you that you do not need them, when you do. That is when you get defensive. Humility is the way out.

Another path around the ego trap is to teach others. Teaching exposes you to a wide range of possibilities in your field and pushes you to learn more so you can be a better guide.

Lying to yourself about what you know blocks growth. Ego tells you that you know it all. The answer is a careful self-check. Frank Shamrock, a mixed martial arts legend, put forward Shamrock's formula to help us manage our egos. The formula calls for building an environment that lifts your abilities. You need a minus, someone you are teaching, and a plus, someone better than you. You also need an equal, someone whose skills are close to yours, for balance. These different relationships sharpen you and keep you aware of what you know and what still needs work. Stay alert and realistic, since a false self-image is corrosive.

chapters.title: Passion alone will not free you from ego
chapters.text:
Have you noticed the world shouting about finding your passion? Do not overrate passion. We hear it tied to success, but it often shows up in failure too. The climbers on Everest in 1996 were passionate, and that did not save them. There is more to it than passion, including a reason, a direction, a purpose, a stack of skills, and hard work.

Passion usually hides a weakness. Its breathless, hurried energy is a poor stand-in for discipline, for mastery, for strength, for purpose, and for steady effort.

Many people lean on their passion without any purpose or sense of reality. Purpose is like passion with edges. It opens you up to far more than just being blinded by the rush in your blood. Purpose and realism are about seeing what the work takes and gaining the angle you need to reach what you want. One spoke in the wheel of a purposeful life is pride. Pride makes you cling to past wins. Truly successful people know they cannot stop learning. They keep pushing and try to top their last win. If Steve Jobs had let his success with the Apple II go to his head, we probably would not have iPhones and iPads today.

Pride will not just keep you from improving, it will make you resistant to correction. Benjamin Franklin once hit his head on a low door frame in Boston, his hometown. The painful moment could have been avoided if he had not been proud. Cotton Mather, one of the most respected figures in town, had warned Franklin about the low frame, but he would not listen. Franklin thought he knew better, and his pride left him hurt.

To break through pride, ask how a humble person would handle the situation, and follow that example. Build new meaningful tasks instead of holding on to past wins. A supportive friend can serve as an accountability partner.

Sometimes, people use more aggressive methods to defend the false image they have of themselves.

chapters.title: When different strengths come together, output grows
chapters.text:
Most great figures in history, including Michelangelo, Leonardo da Vinci, and Benjamin Franklin, started by serving other successful people. It is called the Apprentice Model. Serving others should not shrink your self-worth, and it does not make you less important. Learning from someone is useful in the early stretch of your career, especially if you find a way to accept it. Acceptance makes room to take the most from the moment or to turn it to your advantage.

Join groups and build ties with people who are already successful. It helps you strengthen your own sense of self as everyone moves forward together.

Many organizations and successful people understand the value of helping others gain insight, so they welcome interns and mentees. The idea is similar to the role of the anteambulo from the Roman art world, which means one who clears the way. The role was about helping someone focus on their strengths and pursue their purpose. An anteambulo was usually a thinker, writer, or artist who served a wealthy patron by clearing their path, both physical and philosophical. Successful business people and politicians paid writers, thinkers, and artists to make works of art or to perform for them in exchange for protection, food, and gifts.

Beyond helping others find their strengths, assigning tasks is also a way to manage your ego. You can get a fresh angle on many problems when you delegate. Ego makes people do things they could have asked others to do. The feeling that you are the only one who can do it right does not serve you. By learning to delegate, you let others grow and use their time well. Failing to trust while handing out work can damage your business and keep you from a life you enjoy.

Delegation frees you to focus on new and important work that will pay off more. As you work with others to reach success, you also have to learn self-control. Whatever we aim to gain depends on what we are willing to put up with. Anger, emotion, and loss of restraint are a recipe for failure. No matter how talented, rich, or connected you are, hold your ego back and do not fight for the sake of fighting. It will save your relationships and help you succeed.

chapters.title: Failure can become a fresh launch
chapters.text:
Rejection can bruise your ego. Expecting a reward you have not earned can sour into frustration. Life will not always bend your way, but the rough stretch you are in can act as a springboard. Rather than letting disappointment fill your head, channel that feeling into thinking about what you can do to lift the situation. Have you ever seen a setback as a chance to change? It can be, once you accept that you only control your effort, not the outcome.

Ego makes you take a neutral or even indifferent situation personally. You are one of nearly eight billion people on the planet, alongside billions of objects and abstract things. Bad, complex things can simply happen, and they should not define your sense of self, since not everything happens because of you. Of course, your ego will shout in your ear, telling you that it is not fair, that you are not enough, that you should quit, that you should not have started. In those moments, remind yourself that unexpected difficulties can come from billions of unpredictable forces that shape your fate. You can be strong and brave enough to handle all of it. Do not break your self-esteem on your own, since you will need it for future wins.

Also, take an honest read of your performance. Hate is the easy feeling while failing. Hate blames and makes others pay for your mistakes, while love is open, egoless, positive, and productive. The choice between them shapes how quickly you bounce back.

Hardship is part of being human. We have to feel the sting of failure at some point. Many people learn from failure but hardly learn from victory.

We all live inside a loop. Wanting leads to victory and misfortune. Victory brings fresh challenges and goals. Those challenges can fuel more rise if you face them head on.

No one is without error. Hardship is part of every path. Try to do better than your best, and your chances of winning grow.

When things do not go your way, dig into the cause of the failure and treat the cause, not just the feeling. Then pick yourself up and try the next small step.

chapters.title: Ego works against what you build
chapters.text:
You start to feel better when you separate yourself from situations and from other people's opinions. The ego tells you that you are invincible and that your power is unlimited. Bring sobriety into your life. Stay self-aware about who you are and what you have yet to do. Sobriety means mastering yourself, and you should embrace it. Staying sober means not being blinded by ego, current status, or power. The money in your account or the recognition you have are not measures of your worth either.

Greatness comes from humble beginnings and from grunt work. It means you are the least important person in the room until you change that with results.

Keeping sobriety means fighting the forces that feed your ego. Ego clouds the mind, but sobriety keeps that from happening. Sobriety balances your rise when things start to get better. It means finding the middle ground between your ego and your drive to achieve.

Happiness and fulfillment sit at the heart of every dream, but when egoism sets in, you lose track of what you really want.

Growing up and getting organized is a strong way to keep ego sobriety. You have to be able to manage yourself and others. Watch out for the disease called me. It starts with thinking you are better or unique and that your problems and experiences are unlike anyone else's. The attitude is selfish and blocks learning.

When someone clears a hurdle, they can feel like Clark Kent, a superhero who saved the world with no help. The truth is that even superheroes have sidekicks. Clark Kent had Lois Lane and many others who helped him be Superman.

Humility will not hurt your career. Appreciating your friends, colleagues, teachers, and trainers is good. Some fields do call for individual brilliance, but still, respect your fans and well-wishers by thanking them warmly for their support. Those who voted for you, bought you your first training kit, brought you coffee while you worked, or fed your cat when you were out of town are all part of your rise. Be humble enough to see them and value their effort. That posture keeps ego quiet while your work speaks for you, which is the real win.

Humility is not weakness. Sobriety is not cold. Both are the calm center from which smart moves come. When you can sit in that center, your future self will thank you for the steady hands you showed along the way.

The author, Ryan Holiday, has a line that drives this home. He writes that greatness grows from humble soil, not from the spotlight. Sit with that idea for a beat, then move on to the next chapter with a lighter step.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
The ego lives in every human being. It grows without your noticing, but you can make conscious efforts to hold it back. When you succeed at something, watch for the moment ego pokes its head up. If left untamed, ego can lead to painful outcomes. It is the enemy in matters of aspiration, success, and failure. Each person has to notice ego's moves in their own life and try to cut them off. Letting ego fester can keep you from your potential and from living truly, happily, and lovingly.

While you chase success, remember the value of having a purpose. Knowing that you are part of something larger fills your success with meaning. When you lose the thread of a higher purpose, success can feel empty, like a part of your soul is gone. Pour into relationships, since they will help you get closer to your goals.

No matter what you have done, you have to be a student for life. You have to keep learning from everyone and everything. The human impulse weaves stories out of past events and adds details that were not there. When you look at quick wins, you credit yourself too soon with powers you cannot control. Face the facts instead of stories. Listen to the feedback that can help you improve and grow.

Your life is the sum of all the choices you make. You are making the right call by deciding that ego is the enemy. You are choosing humility, knowledge, and self-control, which are core to your road to success.

Try this. Hand a task you do well to a few different people and see what you can learn from their work. Also, pick an area of interest and use Shamrock's formula to sharpen your skills and clear ego from your life.
